Title: Database DevOps: CD for Stateful Applications
Abstract: Running stateful applications can provide many of the same advantages as stateless applications. In this talk, Stephen will share some thoughts on managing stateful applications as part of a CD Pipeline so that applications - and the application's data - can be versioned and deployed safely and repeatedly. This talk will discuss managing structural changes to a database as part of a CD process. The talk will dive into automation approaches and tooling for managing data migrations between environments and running database migrations within a CI/CD pipeline. The talk will feature real-world examples where we discuss specific schema migrations, their possible performance impacts and downtime implications. We will demonstrate how a complex migration can be done with 0 downtime. With AI and CI/CD we can provide something better than before: A more testable, repeatable, and open way to deploy stateful applications. This talk features a practical demo of how CD tooling can empower users to automate data migrations within Kubernetes.

Title: Cloud-Native Data Architectures for Real-Time Marketing Analytics in Database DevOps
Abstract: Modern marketing analytics systems are increasingly constrained not by data volume, but by the ability to process, store, and act on data in real time. This talk explores how cloud-native database architectures and DevOps practices are transforming marketing analytics from batch-driven pipelines into real-time, event-driven systems capable of millisecond-level responsiveness.  Drawing from real-world implementation patterns, we examine how organizations design and operate scalable data platforms that process millions of customer events per second using distributed data stores, streaming pipelines, and cloud-managed database services. The session will highlight how teams reduced data processing latency by over 60% and improved query performance by more than 70% by adopting modern database technologies and automated infrastructure workflows.  We will walk through practical architecture decisions, including the use of data lakes vs. data warehouses, stream processing frameworks, and real-time indexing strategies for dynamic audience segmentation. Attendees will also learn how continuous integration and deployment (CI/CD) pipelines are applied to data systems, enabling seamless schema evolution, zero-downtime model retraining, and automated scaling without service disruption.  The talk will cover lessons learned from building and operating these systems in production—what worked, what failed, and how teams addressed challenges in observability, data consistency, and cost optimization. We will also explore emerging trends such as federated learning for privacy-aware analytics and AutoML integration within data pipelines.  This session is ideal for engineers and architects looking to bridge the gap between database systems and DevOps practices, with concrete examples of how to build reliable, scalable, and high-performance real-time analytics platforms.

Title: AI-Driven Database Modernization and Hybrid Cloud DevOps for Scalable Healthcare Systems
Abstract: Modern healthcare systems increasingly rely on complex, data-intensive applications, yet many organizations still operate legacy database infrastructures that limit scalability, observability, and operational efficiency. This session explores how AI-driven database modernization combined with hybrid cloud DevOps practices enables healthcare platforms to build, deploy, and operate resilient, high-performance systems. Drawing from real-world implementations, we examine how automated schema optimization, intelligent query tuning, and AI-assisted anomaly detection improve database performance and reliability in production environments.  We also highlight how hybrid cloud architectures support workload portability while meeting strict compliance and data governance requirements. Practical insights include lessons learned from migrating legacy electronic health record systems to cloud-native databases, implementing CI/CD pipelines for database changes, and enhancing observability through integrated monitoring and logging frameworks. Attendees will gain an understanding of trade-offs in architectural decisions, including cost versus performance and consistency versus scalability.  Additionally, we discuss how automation and DevOps workflows reduce deployment friction, improve incident response times, and enable continuous delivery of data-driven features. This talk provides actionable strategies, tools, and best practices for engineers and architects seeking to modernize database operations and build reliable, scalable healthcare systems using AI and hybrid cloud technologies.

Title: Can a Kubernetes Operator Replace a DBA? A 30-Day Experiment.
Abstract: Kubernetes operators promise to automate complex operational tasks, backups, failovers, upgrades, scaling, and turn database management into a declarative workflow. But how far does that promise actually go?  In this talk, I run a 30-day experiment to find out.  Using a PostgreSQL cluster deployed with the CloudNativePG operator, I handed over day-to-day database operations to Kubernetes and observed what happened in real-world conditions. Over the course of a month, I monitored the system while intentionally introducing common production problems: node failures, pod crashes, storage pressure, replication issues, upgrades, and backup recovery scenarios.  Some problems were resolved automatically within seconds. Others exposed surprising limitations where manual intervention was still required.  This talk walks through the experiment week by week, showing what the operator handled gracefully, what broke, and where human operators are still essential. Along the way we’ll explore the operational model behind Kubernetes operators, the trade-offs of automated database management, and the practical lessons teams should know before trusting automation with their stateful workloads.  If you’ve ever wondered whether operators can truly replace traditional database administration, or simply shift the role, this experiment provides a candid, data-driven answer.  Key Takeaways  Attendees will learn:  - How Kubernetes operators manage stateful systems like PostgreSQL - Which database failures operators can automatically recover from - Where manual operational knowledge is still required - The operational trade-offs of running databases in Kubernetes - Practical lessons from a month-long real-world experiment

Title: AI-Powered Resilience and Observability in Database DevOps Multi-Cloud Architectures
Abstract: Modern database-driven systems operating in multi-cloud environments face increasing complexity in ensuring performance, reliability, and operational efficiency. As organizations adopt Database DevOps practices to accelerate delivery and maintain system integrity, traditional reactive monitoring approaches struggle to keep pace with dynamic workloads and distributed architectures. This talk presents an AI-powered framework for enhancing resilience, observability, and automation in database-centric DevOps ecosystems.  The proposed approach leverages machine learning techniques, including anomaly detection and time-series forecasting, to continuously analyze database telemetry, query performance metrics, and infrastructure signals across heterogeneous cloud platforms. By identifying early indicators of performance degradation, resource contention, and potential system failures, the framework enables proactive intervention before issues impact end users. Real-world implementations demonstrate significant improvements, including reduced mean time to detection and resolution, optimized resource utilization, and enhanced system uptime.  A key focus of this session is practical applicability. We will explore how AI integrates with existing DevOps tooling such as Kubernetes, CI/CD pipelines, and database orchestration platforms to enable self-healing capabilities and intelligent workload distribution. Attendees will gain insights into architectural decisions, including cross-provider data normalization, observability pipelines, and automated remediation strategies tailored for database environments.  Additionally, the talk highlights lessons learned from deploying predictive resilience systems, including challenges in debugging distributed failures, balancing automation with human oversight, and ensuring security and compliance in multi-cloud database operations. Case study examples will illustrate how teams improved scalability, reduced operational friction, and enhanced system transparency through incremental adoption of AI-driven practices.  This session is designed for engineers and practitioners seeking actionable strategies to build, operate, and scale reliable database systems. Attendees will leave with concrete techniques to improve observability, automate workflows, and implement resilient Database DevOps practices in real-world environments.

Title: NLB Generative Frameworks for Scalable Database E2E Testing
Abstract: Database DevOps teams face a silent crisis. Traditional automation frameworks built on hardcoded scripts and brittle locators consume up to 70% of QA engineering time in pure maintenance, time that should go toward validating schema migrations, data pipeline integrity, and release-critical database flows. This talk shares lessons learned building Autonomous Cognitive Navigation (ACN), a Natural Language Based (NLB) generative framework that replaces script-driven testing with intent-based reasoning, designed for the complexity of modern database DevOps pipelines. At its core is a State-Aware Reasoning Loop powered by a Large Language Model that interprets real-time application state, maintains a full chronological action history, and resolves high-entropy flows spanning 20+ steps from a single natural language instruction. Three engineering decisions shaped its reliability: a Persistent Temporal Action Memory that eliminated infinite-loop failures seen in earlier embedding-based approaches; a Semantic Canonicalization function that reduced UI view hierarchy token volume by 85% for faster and more accurate reasoning; and a multimodal end-state verification engine that stays resilient to the UI and schema changes that routinely break identifier-based checks. In practice: 95% reliability across thousands of application variants and 70+ languages, compared to near-100% failure rates of legacy scripts after a UI update. New test flows dropped from 120 hours of manual scripting to under 4 hours of natural language definition, saving an estimated 27 developer-years of maintenance effort and enabling a 15 to 20% increase in feature release velocity. Attendees will leave with concrete techniques they can evaluate and apply to their own database testing and CI/CD workflows.

Title: From 0 to 150k Users in 3 Months: What Actually Breaks in Your APIs
Abstract: Scaling a product from 0 to 150k users in three months doesn’t just increase traffic, it exposes every weak assumption in your system.  In this talk, I’ll walk through the real API and architecture challenges we faced during rapid growth, and what we had to change to keep the system stable. This isn’t a story about frameworks or hype. It’s a breakdown of what actually breaks when your APIs move from “it works locally” to handling real-world usage at scale.  We’ll explore how small inconsistencies and assumptions in API design compound across services, why traditional middleware patterns become a bottleneck, and how documentation and testing quickly drift from reality under pressure. I’ll also share how we restructured our request pipeline, enforced consistency across services, and improved performance without slowing down development.  Using ElysiaJS as part of our stack, we were able to simplify cross-cutting concerns, reduce overhead, and bring our APIs closer to predictable, low-latency behavior. But more importantly, we’ll focus on the system design decisions that made the biggest difference, independent of tooling.  I would also go into talks about how we wrote a custom query builder for postgres and mysql, so our apis could share similar query contracts, and how we borrowed ideas like meters from stripe, or WAL to store user event and how that simplified storing and returning very important responses.  If you’re building or maintaining APIs in a fast-growing product, this talk will give you a practical view of what to expect, what to avoid, and what actually holds up under scale.  Why this talk?  Most talks about scaling focus on infrastructure, databases, or queues. But in many systems, the first point of failure is the API layer itself.  This talk focuses on the often-overlooked layer where design decisions, performance, and developer experience intersect, and shows how to build APIs that can survive rapid growth without collapsing under their own complexity.

Title: Automating Database Operations with Cortex Code
Abstract: With Cortex Code and the power of DBT, SQL, Python and autonomous agents we can easily manage and build advanced automation, continuous integration, continuous deployment and version control.   By using Snowflake authored skills, Cortex Code CLI allows for natural language to manage schema creation and change, with full testing and automated deployments.

Title: Schema Migrations in CI/CD: When AI Helps, When It Hurts, and How to Tell the Difference
Abstract: Schema migrations are the riskiest deploy most teams ship. Application releases can be rolled forward and back. A migration that locks a 2TB table for an hour, or quietly loses data on rollback, cannot. And now AI wants to write them for us. This talk is a working CI/CD field guide for shipping database changes safely in cloud environments, and an honest assessment of where AI-assisted review fits inside that pipeline. We'll cover the migration patterns that hold up: backward-compatible schema changes, expand-and-contract releases, automated migration tests, approval gates, and post-deploy verification. Then we'll add the AI layer where it earns its keep, including drafting forward and rollback DDL, flagging unsafe operations before review (full-table rewrites, locks held in transactions, missing concurrency hints), and triaging incidents when a migration goes sideways. I'll also be direct about where AI breaks down. Hallucinated database syntax, like Postgres flags that are actually MySQL flags. Missing the operational context that a "trivial" migration on a hot table will lock production for an hour. Rollback scripts that compile but quietly lose rows. The framework, three axes for scoring any migration workflow on blast radius, reversibility, and human-in-loop requirement, gives engineers a defensible way to decide where automation and AI belong in their pipeline, and where they don't. Attendees leave with concrete CI/CD patterns they can adopt next sprint, the specific failure modes to design around, and a practical decision rule for keeping engineering judgement in control of production data.

Title: Continuous Compliance in SaaS: Automating Scalable DevOps Delivery
Abstract: As SaaS systems scale, maintaining compliance without slowing delivery is a core DevOps challenge. This talk presents a data-driven framework for continuous compliance across the SaaS lifecycle, showing how automation integrates governance into development and operations. Drawing from enterprise environments with high uptime and significant efficiency gains, we explore how teams embed compliance from design through deployment using automated testing, security scanning, and CI/CD pipelines. We’ll examine measurable benchmarks for reliability, performance, and resource efficiency, along with practical approaches to aligning with standards like SOX and VPAT. Attendees will learn how database and infrastructure automation enable consistent compliance, reduce operational overhead, and support sustainable, scalable SaaS delivery.

Title: AI Meets SAP: Building Real Time Pricing Optimization Pipelines Beyond Rule Based Systems
Abstract: Traditional SAP pricing relies on static rules that struggle to keep up with dynamic market conditions and evolving customer behavior. As organizations adopt modern data platforms and DevOps practices, pricing remains one of the least modernized components in the enterprise stack.  This session explores how to transform pricing into a real time, data driven system using AI and modern integration patterns. We walk through a hybrid architecture where SAP handles transactional processing and financial validation, while an external AI engine fed by continuously updated data pipelines delivers optimized pricing, discount strategies, and risk adjusted recommendations.  Using API based integration and SAP BAdI extensibility, the solution maintains clean core compliance while enabling rapid iteration, model deployment, and observability aligned with Database DevOps and modern data engineering principles.  We discuss how to design real time pricing pipelines using enterprise data sources, integrate AI services with SAP without breaking core systems, and operationalize machine learning models in production with strong DevOps practices. We also address consistency, auditability, and performance at scale.  Based on real enterprise deployments, this approach significantly reduced manual interventions and enabled responsive, market aware pricing decisions. This session provides a practical blueprint for turning static ERP logic into intelligent, continuously evolving decision systems.

Title: Automating Healthcare Data Pipelines with Database DevOps
Abstract: Healthcare organizations rely on complex data ecosystems where clinical, billing, and operational systems must remain synchronized to ensure accurate patient care and financial operations. Managing healthcare coding standards such as ICD-10, CPT, and ASA codes, along with processing patient payment data, often involves manual database updates and data reconciliation tasks that introduce delays, inconsistencies, and operational risk.  This session explores how Database DevOps practices can modernize healthcare revenue cycle data management through automated data pipelines and continuous database integration. The presentation demonstrates how ETL workflows, version-controlled database deployments, and automated validation frameworks can streamline updates across electronic medical record systems, billing platforms, and enterprise data warehouses.  Attendees will learn how DevOps-driven database automation supports reliable data synchronization, schema management, and monitoring across multiple systems. The session also highlights automated processing of payment and billing data from external portals, including validation, reconciliation, and exception handling. By applying Database DevOps principles, organizations can improve data reliability, reduce manual database operations, and build scalable, maintainable data platforms that support healthcare analytics and revenue cycle performance.

Title: When Database Risk Stays Hidden: Closing the Visibility Gap in Data Operations
Abstract: Database DevOps is no longer limited to backups, deployments, migrations, monitoring, or performance tuning. As systems move from clusters to constellations, the real risk is often not that teams lack data; it is that critical database signals do not reach the right people in time.  This practical session reframes confidence as a measurable operational communication competency, not a personality trait. Using the VISIBLE™ Framework — Voice, Identity, Spotlight, Inner Work, Belief, Leverage, and Elevation — participants will examine how visibility gaps affect incident response, migration planning, reliability reviews, performance conversations, postmortems, and cross-functional trust.  Attendees will leave with a repeatable framework for surfacing database concerns earlier, translating technical risk into decision-ready language, and strengthening communication between DBAs, SREs, DevOps teams, engineers, product leaders, and business stakeholders. In Database DevOps, visibility is not self-promotion; it is reliability infrastructure.  By the end of this session, participants will be able to: 	1.	Identify three visibility gaps that can affect database reliability, incident response, migration risk, performance tuning, and stakeholder trust. 	2.	Apply the VISIBLE™ Framework to assess how voice, identity, spotlight, inner work, belief, leverage, and elevation influence database operations communication. 	3.	Design a practical communication strategy that helps teams surface database risks, explain tradeoffs, and move operational insight to action earlier.

Title: From Data Streams to Decisions: AI-Powered Operator Assistants for Real-Time Manufacturing Intelligence
Abstract: Modern manufacturing environments generate massive, high-velocity data streams—often exceeding 500 sensor readings per second per production line. Yet, despite this abundance, critical operational decisions are still frequently driven by fragmented systems, static documentation, and experience-based tribal knowledge.  This session introduces AI-powered Virtual Operator Assistants (VOAs) as a new paradigm for bridging the gap between raw data and real-time decision-making. By combining natural language interfaces, semantic search, and domain-tuned foundation models, VOAs enable operators to interact with complex manufacturing data ecosystems as intuitively as querying a colleague.  We’ll examine how organizations have achieved 4–5x faster troubleshooting (reducing resolution times from 45–90 minutes to under 20 minutes) and 60–75% reductions in training time by deploying VOAs built on modern data platforms such as Amazon Kendra and Bedrock. These systems unify structured and unstructured data—including maintenance logs, CAD artifacts, and legacy manuals—into searchable, context-aware knowledge layers, often digitized through OCR and integrated into scalable data pipelines.  Through real-world case studies from semiconductor fabs and discrete manufacturing environments, we’ll explore key DevOps and data challenges: versioning of operational knowledge, real-time data ingestion, trust calibration in AI outputs, and designing resilient interfaces for noisy shop-floor conditions. In one deployment, documentation retrieval time dropped from 30 minutes to under a minute, while cross-site operational consistency improved significantly.  We’ll also look ahead to emerging capabilities such as digital twin integration, predictive diagnostics based on telemetry drift, and voice-driven workflows, all powered by continuously evolving data pipelines and AI models.  This talk positions AI not as a replacement for human expertise, but as a force multiplier—capturing decades of institutional knowledge and making it instantly accessible. Attendees will gain a practical blueprint for implementing VOAs, including architecture patterns, DevOps integration strategies, and measurable ROI frameworks for transitioning to machine-augmented operations.

Title: Zero-Shot Self-Improving NER for Cyber Threat Intelligence Pipelines
Abstract: Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) is critical for proactive and resilient cybersecurity, yet most intelligence is published as unstructured text, limiting its integration into data pipelines and operational systems. Named Entity Recognition (NER) is a key technique for transforming such reports into structured, queryable data, but existing approaches struggle with domain-specific terminology and inconsistent outputs from large language models—especially in low-resource, rapidly evolving environments.  This work presents a zero-shot, self-improving NER framework designed for CTI workflows in modern data and DevOps ecosystems. The approach combines automated domain knowledge injection with an iterative reasoning process, enabling large language models to refine predictions without manual annotation or retraining.  The framework consists of four components: a domain knowledge extractor that enriches prompts with cybersecurity context, a data annotator that generates multiple candidate outputs, a consistency filter that removes unreliable annotations, and a self-retrieval reasoner that reuses high-confidence outputs as contextual examples for future inference.  This iterative loop improves entity extraction quality while reducing hallucinations and variability. Evaluations across multiple benchmark datasets under strict zero-shot conditions show consistent gains over prompt-based baselines in both accuracy and stability.  By producing structured, high-quality entities without labeled data, this approach enables scalable ingestion of CTI into databases and analytics systems. It supports reliable downstream querying, automation, and integration into DevOps pipelines, making it practical for real-world security operations and data-driven defense strategies.

JobTitle1: Product Lead, Camera Intelligence Edge AI
Title: Building Privacy-First AI Vision Systems: Lessons from a Context-Aware Architecture in Production
Abstract: Deploying AI vision systems in real-world environments introduces not just model challenges, but complex data, privacy, and system design problems. In this talk, we share practical lessons from building and operating a privacy-first AI vision pipeline across a mixed-use urban environment.  We walk through a production architecture that separates context detection, privacy enforcement, and vision processing into independent services, connected via APIs and event-driven pipelines. The system uses edge processing for real-time anonymization, temporary pseudonymous identifiers for session tracking, and policy engines to enforce privacy rules dynamically based on location and use case.  The session focuses on engineering decisions: how we handled data flow across services, optimized latency with edge vs. cloud trade-offs, and ensured compliance without breaking downstream analytics. We’ll also cover observability strategies for privacy-sensitive systems, including logging without exposing personal data, and debugging issues in distributed pipelines.  A key part of the talk is what didn’t work—early design mistakes, performance bottlenecks, and challenges in maintaining consistency across services. We’ll share how we improved reliability, reduced system complexity, and automated policy enforcement.  Attendees will leave with concrete patterns for building scalable, privacy-aware data systems, along with insights into trade-offs between performance, privacy, and operational complexity in modern AI pipelines.

Title: Responsible Agentic AI for Equitable and Sustainable Telecom DevOps
Abstract: Artificial intelligence is reshaping telecommunications infrastructure across 5G and emerging 6G ecosystems, where intelligent systems manage spectrum allocation, automated fault detection, traffic forecasting, and reinforcement learning driven power optimization. As these capabilities become embedded within database driven network operations and DevOps pipelines, they influence not only performance and availability but also equity, governance, and environmental impact.  Globally, 4.0 billion people have high speed connectivity, yet 1.5 billion remain under connected and 1.1 billion remain completely unconnected. AI powered resource allocation models trained on historically uneven investment data risk reinforcing disparities by prioritizing high revenue urban regions over rural and economically disadvantaged communities. When automation operates without fairness constraints, database level decisions can institutionalize algorithmic redlining at scale.  Environmental trade offs also emerge within AI enabled operations. Reinforcement learning can reduce idle base station energy consumption through demand aware transmission adjustments, and digital twin simulations allow infrastructure strategies to be tested before deployment. However, continuous model training, inference workloads, and data storage pipelines introduce significant computational overhead. The sustainability outcome depends on whether operational efficiency gains outweigh AI’s infrastructure footprint.  This session proposes a governance aligned DevOps framework that embeds transparency, accountability, fairness validation, and carbon reporting directly into AI driven network optimization workflows. By integrating auditability, allocation monitoring, and measurable sustainability metrics into database and deployment processes, telecommunications providers can ensure that agent based automation advances reliability while promoting equitable access and environmental responsibility.

Title: AI-Driven Automation for Scalable Enterprise Development Workflows
Abstract: Enterprise web development has grown increasingly complex with the adoption of microservices architectures, cloud native infrastructure, API driven ecosystems, and distributed data platforms. Organizations now operate hundreds of services and APIs supported by extensive CI/CD pipelines across multi cloud environments. While these architectures enable scalability and rapid delivery, they also introduce operational and cognitive challenges for engineering teams responsible for maintaining reliable development workflows.  This presentation explores how artificial intelligence is emerging as a practical productivity multiplier within enterprise development environments. The session begins by examining AI powered knowledge intelligence systems that unify fragmented documentation across code repositories, architecture diagrams, ticketing systems, and internal knowledge bases. Through semantic search and retrieval augmented generation, developers can query organizational knowledge conversationally and quickly retrieve contextual information from distributed documentation sources.  The talk then examines AI augmented development environments that assist engineers by generating service scaffolding, validating API contracts, suggesting refactoring opportunities, and identifying potential security vulnerabilities. These capabilities reduce time spent on repetitive development tasks while improving code quality and architectural consistency across distributed systems.  The session also highlights AI enabled testing and developer onboarding workflows. Machine learning driven QA systems can analyze code paths and historical defect patterns to generate targeted test scenarios and prioritize high risk modules in CI/CD pipelines. In parallel, AI guided onboarding assistants help new engineers interpret architectural documentation and understand service dependencies within complex enterprise platforms.  The presentation concludes by demonstrating how structured human AI collaboration can strengthen enterprise development workflows while maintaining engineering oversight and system reliability.

Title: Building Your First RAG System: The Gap Between Demo and Production
Abstract: The demo always works. You upload some PDFs, wire up an LLM, and suddenly you have a chatbot that seems to know everything about your company, then you ship it, and things get interesting...  Retrieval-Augmented Generation sounds straightforward until you are six weeks into a production deployment and your system is confidently hallucinating old API endpoints. The gap between a working demo and a reliable production system is not a gap. It's a canyon.  In this talk, we walk through the three core components of a RAG architecture: the embedding pipeline, the vector store, and the retrieval and generation layer. For each one, we cover what the docs tell you and what production actually looks like.  - Chunking strategies for technical documentation, including why chunk size is not a set-and-forget decision - Vector store options (Pinecone, Qdrant, pgvector) and the operational tradeoffs that actually matter at scale - Prompt engineering patterns that prevent hallucination without sacrificing usefulness - The four failure modes that will not show up in your monitoring but will show up in your tickets - Infrastructure-as-code patterns for managing RAG deployments, including the parts Terraform fundamentally cannot help with - Document freshness pipelines and why stale embeddings are worse than no embeddings at all  By the end of this session, you will understand not just how to build a RAG system, but how to operate one. You will know where it breaks, what the symptoms look like, and how to build ingestion pipelines that keep your system from serving incorrect information to the people who trust it most.  This is a practitioner talk drawn from real production deployments, not a vendor demo or a tutorial built on toy data.

Title: From One Model to Many: Operationalizing ML at Scale with DevOps & MLOps
Abstract: As enterprises scale their machine learning initiatives, managing a handful of models quickly evolves into orchestrating dozens—or even hundreds—across teams, environments, and business functions. This shift introduces complex challenges around versioning, deployment consistency, governance, and operational reliability—areas where traditional data science workflows fall short.  This session explores how DevOps principles, combined with modern MLOps frameworks, enable organizations to manage ML models as scalable, governed, and production-grade assets. Drawing on real-world patterns from industries such as finance and telecommunications, we examine how teams operationalize use cases like fraud detection, credit scoring, customer segmentation, and predictive maintenance through standardized pipelines, centralized model registries, and automated monitoring systems.  Key topics include strategies for model lifecycle management, handling drift and retraining at scale, ensuring auditability and compliance, and establishing clear ownership across distributed teams. We will also dive into architectural patterns and tooling approaches that bring consistency to model deployment and reduce duplication of effort.  Attendees will gain practical insights into building resilient, scalable ML operations aligned with DevOps best practices—enabling faster iteration, reduced risk, and sustained business value. This session is ideal for engineers, data platform teams, and ML practitioners looking to bridge the gap between experimentation and enterprise-grade production systems.

Title: Unified DOCSIS Chipset Development: Simplifying Multi-Vendor Deployment, Interoperability, and Network Performance
Abstract: As broadband operators modernize DOCSIS networks, supporting multi-vendor environments at scale introduces significant operational complexity. Differences in chipset behavior can affect channel bonding, modulation profiles, error correction, diagnostics, and overall service reliability, making deployment and troubleshooting harder across lab, field, and production environments.  This session explores how a unified DOCSIS chipset development approach can reduce that complexity by standardizing key functions across devices and vendors. It will examine how greater consistency in upstream and downstream processing, dynamic channel management, higher-order modulation support, firmware behavior, and observability can improve interoperability and make network operations more predictable.  The presentation will also cover the practical impact of chipset standardization on validation pipelines, field trials, rollout readiness, centralized monitoring, and incident response. By reducing implementation variability, operators can streamline deployment workflows, accelerate troubleshooting, and improve coordination across engineering, operations, and field teams.  Finally, the session will connect chipset unification to longer-term network evolution, including high-split architectures, next-generation DOCSIS readiness, and more scalable broadband infrastructure. Attendees will leave with a practical framework for using chipset standardization to strengthen performance consistency, improve operational efficiency, and build a more reliable foundation for future network transformation.

Title: Semantic Documentation Retrieval with MCP, ChromaDB, and RAG Pipelines
Abstract: Enterprise software teams often rely on large internal component libraries, yet documentation discoverability remains a persistent barrier to developer productivity. In large design systems with numerous components, configuration options, dependencies, and integration patterns, fragmented documentation frequently leads developers to recreate existing components or misuse available ones.  This session presents a production implementation of a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server integrated with ChromaDB vector databases and LangChain-powered retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipelines to enable semantic documentation retrieval within AI-assisted development workflows. Using transformer-based embeddings, component specifications, usage examples, and design tokens are stored as vector representations in ChromaDB, enabling efficient similarity search combined with metadata filtering for precise contextual responses.  The system architecture includes a structured query pipeline consisting of natural language embedding, semantic similarity search, relevance ranking, and contextual response generation with code snippets and related component suggestions. Integrated with GitHub Copilot, the system aligns AI-generated code recommendations with internal component standards rather than generic public examples.  Operational outcomes include reduced documentation-related context switching, improved component reuse, fewer redundant implementations, faster developer onboarding, and improved architectural consistency across distributed engineering teams.  Attendees will gain practical insights into deploying MCP servers, structuring ChromaDB collections, designing semantic retrieval pipelines, and operationalizing intelligent documentation systems that transform internal knowledge into a scalable engineering asset.

Title: AI in Hiring: How Data Infrastructure Powers Smarter Talent Decisions
Abstract: Enterprise talent acquisition runs on data infrastructure, and most of it is not built for the job. Traditional applicant tracking systems were designed for administrative workflow, not for the scale, speed, or analytical demands of modern hiring operations. This session shares what it looks like to move beyond that baseline and what the data architecture decisions look like in practice.  We examine five progressive capability layers in enterprise recruiting systems, from legacy ATS deployments to self-optimizing talent pipelines, and walk through the infrastructure that makes each layer possible: skills graph frameworks, candidate matching models, vector databases, and the real-time data pipelines that feed predictive hiring workflows. These are not theoretical constructs. They are design choices with real trade-offs, and this session covers what worked, what did not, and what we would approach differently.  A significant portion of the session focuses on governance and risk. Algorithmic screening tools are under active regulatory scrutiny and litigation, including cases involving major HR platforms. We cover how bias detection, model auditing, and human oversight were built into the system and why those decisions were made, not just what was deployed.  Attendees will leave with a practical maturity framework for evaluating talent acquisition infrastructure, an understanding of the Five Layers of Predictive Talent Acquisition, and governance considerations they can apply when deploying AI in high-stakes automated decision-making.

Title: Doing more with Cloud API's
Abstract: All the major Cloud vendors provide customers with a rich api based capability to manage your environment. In many cases these apis provide more functionality than is available through the Web console, but less easy to use. By extracting api output and loading into a database this allows you to perform complex analysis on that data, and build applications to enhance your Cloud experience

Title: Five Hard Lessons from Five Years of So-Called Serverless Databases
Abstract: After five years of managing serverless databases, I have learned that my rollercoaster journey is very similar to the CPU usage you dream of seeing in the console. This session shares five hard-earned lessons learned while working with so-called serverless databases.

Title: RAG in production: challenges, techniques and solutions
Abstract: You may have seen reports of AI models suggesting eating rocks or putting glue on pizza. RAG (retrieval-augmented generation) is an architecture designed to prevent these kinds of hallucinations. RAG contains two elements: a “retriever” (vector database) to obtain contextual information, and a generator (LLM) to produce the final response. It sounds simple, but implementing and deploying RAG in production has its own challenges. In this talk, you’ll learn about some of these challenges, as well as techniques to mitigate or eliminate them.

Title: Agentic AI for Database DevOps: Intelligent Automation for Modern Data Platforms
Abstract: Database environments are at the heart of modern applications, yet operational knowledge about schemas, migrations, performance tuning, incidents, and compliance is often fragmented across tickets, dashboards, documentation, and monitoring systems. During outages or performance degradation, database and DevOps teams must manually correlate logs, query metrics, deployment history, and historical fixes—slowing resolution and increasing risk to data integrity and SLAs.  This session explores the design and implementation of an Agentic AI-powered assistant built to enhance Database DevOps workflows. By integrating large language models, vector search, and event-driven microservices with database observability and CI/CD pipelines, we transformed static documentation and troubleshooting artifacts into actionable, intelligent workflows. The system provides contextual query analysis, migration insights, runbook guidance, and controlled remediation recommendations while operating within enterprise governance and security constraints.  We will examine architectural patterns for integrating AI with database monitoring systems, automating schema-change intelligence, and scaling knowledge retrieval across hybrid and multi-cloud data platforms. Real-world lessons include handling sensitive data, optimizing performance under load, and aligning automation with compliance requirements.  The results demonstrate measurable reductions in incident resolution time, improved database deployment confidence, and stronger cross-team knowledge reuse. By embedding intelligent automation into database operations, organizations can strengthen reliability, accelerate delivery, and modernize Database DevOps practices with confidence.

Title: AI Driven Database DevOps Architectures for Scalable Cloud Systems
Abstract: Modern enterprises are increasingly challenged to manage data intensive systems that must scale dynamically while maintaining reliability and consistency. This session explores how database DevOps practices combined with AI driven architectures are transforming the way organizations build and operate data platforms across cloud environments.  The discussion begins with how continuous integration and continuous delivery pipelines are evolving to support database schema versioning, automated migrations, and zero downtime deployments. Attendees will learn how event driven architectures and microservices enable decoupled data workflows that improve deployment velocity while preserving data integrity.  The session highlights how AI and machine learning models are being embedded into database operations to optimize query performance, detect anomalies, and automate capacity planning. Real time analytics pipelines process streaming and transactional data, enabling faster decision making and proactive system adjustments.  Special focus is given to hybrid and multi cloud environments where organizations must manage distributed databases across regions while ensuring compliance and resilience. Strategies for replication, failover handling, and workload balancing are discussed in the context of high availability systems.  Beyond infrastructure, the session examines observability frameworks that provide deep visibility into database performance, including metrics, logs, and traces. These insights enable teams to quickly identify bottlenecks and maintain service reliability in complex distributed systems.  Attendees will gain practical knowledge on building scalable database DevOps pipelines, integrating AI into operational workflows, and designing resilient architectures that support continuous delivery. The session provides actionable approaches for engineers and architects aiming to modernize data systems while balancing speed, reliability, and governance in cloud native environments.

Title: From Hallucination to Precision: Building Enterprise-Grade Knowledge Systems with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
Abstract: Enterprise knowledge systems are reaching a critical inflection point. Traditional Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle with hallucinations, outdated training data, and lack of real-time context, making them unreliable for mission-critical applications. In database-driven and DevOps-focused environments, where data accuracy, lineage, and performance are essential, these limitations present significant risks.  Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is emerging as a transformative architecture that addresses these challenges by combining LLMs with real-time, database-backed retrieval systems. By grounding AI outputs in trusted enterprise data sources such as data lakes, vector databases, and operational systems, RAG enables highly accurate, context-aware, and scalable knowledge solutions.  This session explores how RAG is reshaping enterprise knowledge systems through a database and DevOps lens. Drawing from real-world implementations across industries including finance, healthcare, and legal, it demonstrates how organizations are significantly reducing hallucination rates, improving retrieval precision, and accelerating knowledge workflows.  Attendees will gain practical insights into designing RAG architectures that integrate seamlessly with modern data platforms and CI/CD pipelines, leveraging vector databases and indexing strategies for high-relevance retrieval, and optimizing system performance to achieve low-latency responses. The session also covers essential considerations around data quality, governance, and lineage to ensure trustworthy AI outputs, along with strategies for scaling systems to support large user bases with production-grade reliability.  In addition, the talk highlights key DevOps practices such as observability for AI pipelines, continuous evaluation of retrieval performance, and deployment patterns that enable rapid iteration and adoption. Real-world case studies illustrate measurable improvements in customer support automation, compliance monitoring, and enterprise search, demonstrating how RAG drives tangible business value.  As enterprise AI adoption accelerates, this session provides a practical blueprint for building next-generation, database-driven knowledge systems where accuracy, scalability, and trust are engineered from the ground up.

Title: Modernizing the Data Layer: A DevOps Blueprint for Resilient, High-Performance Databases
Abstract: Enterprise modernization efforts often focus on application layers while overlooking the database systems that anchor performance, availability, and data integrity. Aging schemas, tightly coupled dependencies, manual deployment practices, and limited observability create bottlenecks that undermine reliability and slow innovation. In high-transaction environments, these weaknesses surface as latency spikes, failed deployments, schema drift, and prolonged recovery times.  This session introduces a Performance Modernization Blueprint tailored for Database DevOps environments. The framework progresses through Stabilization, Optimization, and Cloud-Native Enablement, integrating automated schema validation, synthetic transaction tracing, distributed caching strategies, event-driven data pipelines, and infrastructure-as-code. By embedding database validation and performance checks directly into CI/CD workflows, organizations can shift from reactive firefighting to continuous reliability engineering.  Real-world implementations demonstrate measurable improvements in availability, query performance, deployment frequency, and mean time to recovery. Containerized database deployments, automated rollback strategies, and observability integration enable reproducible, scalable environments aligned with DevOps principles. The blueprint also addresses migration risks such as undocumented dependencies, data consistency challenges, and cross-team coordination gaps through phased rollouts and KPI-driven governance.  By treating the database as a first-class citizen in DevOps transformation, this approach provides a practical roadmap for building resilient, observable, and cloud-ready data platforms that support mission-critical enterprise workloads.

Title: Cloud Native Data Transformation: DevOps Patterns and Measurable Outcomes
Abstract: Enterprise cloud-native transformation is no longer just application migration—it is a data and database DevOps journey that aligns architecture, operations, and measurable business outcomes. This session presents a structured, pattern-driven approach to modernizing legacy systems into scalable microservices while ensuring data consistency, reliability, and governance.  We explore proven migration patterns such as Strangler Fig, API gateways, and event-driven architectures, with a focus on how they impact database evolution, data flow design, and operational pipelines. Attendees will learn how techniques like change data capture (CDC) and CQRS enable reliable data synchronization across distributed systems while minimizing migration risks.  From a DevOps perspective, the session highlights how Kubernetes and automation frameworks support self-healing systems, autoscaling, and efficient resource utilization, while integrating database changes into CI/CD workflows. Key challenges—including data security, access continuity, and schema evolution—are addressed through practical strategies.  The session also connects architecture to outcomes, demonstrating how organizations improve system performance, optimize resource usage, and enhance reliability through modern DevOps practices. Emphasis is placed on observability, performance benchmarking, and cost efficiency in data-driven environments.  Attendees will leave with a practical blueprint for executing cloud-native transformations that balance speed, resilience, compliance, and operational excellence, with a strong focus on managing data systems at scale.

Title: Building Scalable Data Platforms: Database DevOps, ML Pipelines & Cloud-Native Architecture
Abstract: This talk presents a practical, real-world case study on building and operating a scalable data platform using Database DevOps principles and cloud-native technologies. As data volumes and complexity grow, traditional database management approaches often struggle with performance, reliability, and operational overhead. This session explores how modern DevOps practices can transform data infrastructure into a resilient, automated, and observable system.  The discussion walks through the evolution of a multi-database environment into a unified platform leveraging distributed processing (Apache Spark), cloud services (AWS S3, Redshift), and container orchestration (Kubernetes). It highlights key architectural decisions, including polyglot persistence strategies, automated ETL pipelines, and infrastructure-as-code for reproducible deployments. Real implementation challenges—such as long-running queries, fragmented data sources, and slow model training cycles—are examined alongside the solutions that addressed them.  Special focus is given to performance tuning, observability, and workflow automation using orchestration tools, as well as lessons learned from failures and redesigns. The session also covers testing strategies, data reliability, and security considerations in production environments.  Attendees will gain actionable insights into designing, deploying, and maintaining modern data platforms, along with practical takeaways to improve scalability, reduce operational friction, and enhance system reliability in Database DevOps workflows.

Title: TRM with VXLAN EVPN on Cisco Nexus 9000 for Scalable Multicast in DevOps Data Centers
Abstract: Modern database platforms and data driven services rely heavily on scalable and predictable network infrastructure. In multi tenant data center environments, multicast traffic used for replication, clustering, and distributed messaging must be delivered efficiently while maintaining strict tenant isolation. Tenant Routing Multicast (TRM) combined with VXLAN EVPN on Cisco Nexus 9000 switches provides a practical architecture to address these requirements.  This session examines how TRM aligns multicast forwarding with tenant specific VRFs within VXLAN overlay fabrics. Attendees will learn how multicast traffic can be scoped using VXLAN Network Identifiers (VNIs) to prevent cross tenant traffic exposure while preserving hardware accelerated performance. The talk explains how this design supports secure and scalable multicast delivery in environments where database systems, microservices platforms, and distributed applications depend on reliable data distribution.  We will review standalone and hybrid deployment models and discuss how decentralized and controller assisted approaches influence scalability, convergence behavior, and operational complexity. Practical scenarios will include inter site TRM deployments and the role of border gateway services in enabling bandwidth efficient multicast replication across geographically distributed data center fabrics.  The session will also analyze operational considerations such as resource utilization, control plane scaling, and recovery timelines during network failures. Security and compliance aspects will be covered, including tenant level isolation, access controls, and audit visibility. This talk provides infrastructure and DevOps teams with practical guidance for designing TRM enabled multicast networks that support modern database and data platform architectures.

Title: Real-Time Cloud Analytics in Fintech: Database DevOps for Scale & Risk
Abstract: The financial services industry is undergoing rapid transformation, with cloud data platforms and Database DevOps practices at the center. Financial institutions are modernizing data pipelines to reduce infrastructure costs while improving agility through automation, continuous integration, and real-time processing.  Risk management has evolved significantly with cloud-native analytics enabling real-time fraud detection and credit risk assessment. Database DevOps practices—such as automated schema migrations, version-controlled data workflows, and continuous validation—help reduce manual intervention while strengthening compliance and auditability. During peak trading activity, scalable data platforms process high volumes of market data with low latency, ensuring timely and reliable insights.  Customer experience is also being reshaped. Real-time dashboards and event-driven architectures allow institutions to deliver personalized services at scale. These systems integrate machine learning for predictive analytics and natural language interfaces, making insights accessible across technical and business teams.  From a technology standpoint, cloud-native databases, microservices, container orchestration, and CI/CD pipelines enable resilient, multi-region deployments. Strong data governance and observability ensure consistency, reliability, and regulatory alignment.  As organizations adopt Database DevOps alongside AI-driven analytics, they unlock faster insights, improved risk controls, and scalable data operations—critical capabilities for competing in today’s digital financial landscape.

Title: AI-Driven Database DevOps: From Predictive Tuning to Autonomous Operations in Production
Abstract: Modern database systems are at the heart of DevOps-driven architectures, yet performance tuning, scaling, and reliability management remain highly manual and reactive in many organizations. In this talk, we share practical lessons from implementing AI-driven database optimization techniques in real-world production environments, focusing on what actually works—and what doesn’t—when integrating intelligence into database DevOps workflows.  We explore how machine learning models can be embedded into CI/CD and operational pipelines to enable predictive workload forecasting, allowing teams to proactively allocate resources and avoid performance bottlenecks during peak demand. Through concrete examples, we demonstrate how automated indexing strategies—powered by reinforcement learning—improved query performance for analytical workloads while reducing storage overhead compared to traditional approaches.  The session also dives into AI-based anomaly detection integrated with observability stacks, highlighting how teams reduced incident detection time from minutes to seconds and significantly lowered false alerts. We discuss how these improvements translated into faster debugging, more reliable deployments, and reduced operational toil.  Beyond the successes, we provide an honest look at challenges encountered, including data quality issues, integration with legacy systems, and the effort required to operationalize AI models within existing DevOps toolchains. We outline the trade-offs between automation and control, and what we would approach differently in future implementations.  Finally, we present a practical roadmap toward semi-autonomous database operations, where feedback loops continuously optimize performance with minimal human intervention. Attendees will leave with actionable insights, architecture patterns, and tooling strategies they can apply to improve database performance, scalability, and reliability in their own DevOps environments.

Title: From Runbooks to Self-Healing DB Systems: A Roadmap to Autonomous Ops
Abstract: As modern database systems evolve across cloud-native, distributed, and multi-tenant environments, traditional runbook-driven operations are increasingly inadequate. Database incidents—ranging from query performance degradation to replication lag and scaling bottlenecks—often require rapid, context-aware responses that static procedures cannot deliver. This results in alert fatigue, slower root cause analysis, and increased mean time to resolution (MTTR).  This session presents a practical roadmap for transitioning from static runbooks to AI-driven self-healing systems in database and data platform environments. It begins by outlining the foundational capabilities required for automation at scale, including deep observability across database layers, event correlation, and standardized operational workflows.  Building on these foundations, the talk explores how AI and machine learning techniques—such as anomaly detection, predictive analytics, and intelligent decision-making—can enhance database reliability and performance. A key focus is the design of closed-loop remediation systems that integrate detection, diagnosis, and automated resolution for common database issues, while ensuring safety through governance and policy controls.  The session introduces a phased maturity model, guiding teams from manual database operations to automated workflows and ultimately toward autonomous data systems. It also emphasizes human-AI collaboration, where engineers remain essential for oversight and continuous improvement.  Attendees will gain actionable strategies to reduce operational overhead, improve database resilience, and build self-managing data infrastructure.

Title: Modernizing Identity Pipelines with ELT: Lessons in Scalability, Automation, and DevOps
Abstract: Modern data platforms are undergoing a fundamental shift from traditional ETL to ELT architectures, especially in systems that manage large-scale consumer identity data. This talk shares practical lessons from designing and operating ELT-based identity pipelines in real-world environments, focusing on how teams improved scalability, reliability, and developer workflows using cloud-native data platforms.  We will explore how separating data ingestion from transformation enabled faster iteration, simplified debugging, and reduced operational bottlenecks. The session highlights how SQL-based transformations, orchestration tools, and automated data quality checks were used to streamline workflows and reduce data errors. Concrete examples will demonstrate how teams handled evolving identity resolution logic without reprocessing raw data, improving both performance and flexibility.  In addition, we will cover key DevOps considerations including pipeline observability, failure handling, schema evolution, and cost optimization. Lessons learned from production systems—including what broke, what scaled well, and what required redesign—will provide actionable insights for engineers building similar data-intensive systems.  Attendees will gain a clear understanding of how ELT architectures fit into modern Database DevOps practices, along with practical strategies for building maintainable, scalable, and efficient data pipelines that support real-time and analytical workloads.

Title: From Data Pipelines to AI Products: Operationalizing Machine Learning and Generative AI in Enterprise Decision Platforms
Abstract: Modern enterprises are rapidly evolving from isolated analytics initiatives to fully operational AI-driven decision platforms, but scaling these systems reliably remains a significant challenge. This session explores how organizations can bridge the gap between data engineering, MLOps, and AI productization to deliver production-grade intelligence at scale.  Drawing on real-world implementations across healthcare, marketing analytics, and technology startups, we examine how robust data pipelines, versioned datasets, and automated deployment workflows enable faster time-to-market and improved operational efficiency. The talk highlights how Database DevOps practices such as continuous integration for data, schema evolution management, and pipeline observability form the backbone of scalable AI systems.  We dive into architectures that combine machine learning and generative AI, including large language models and retrieval augmented generation, with a strong focus on reliability, reproducibility, and governance. Attendees will learn how modern data platforms support continuous model experimentation, real-time inference, and seamless integration with transactional and analytical systems.  Key areas include designing resilient cloud native data and machine learning pipelines, implementing MLOps practices for faster and safer model deployments, ensuring data quality and governance in AI workflows, enabling observability across data and model layers, and scaling AI systems for real time decision making.  This session emphasizes a practical DevOps first approach to AI, where data, models, and infrastructure are treated as versioned, testable, and deployable assets. By aligning engineering discipline with AI innovation, organizations can move beyond experimentation to build reliable and scalable AI products that deliver measurable business impact.

Title: Cloud-Native Data Platforms for Trading Systems: Lessons in Performance, Scalability, and Real-Time Analytics
Abstract: Modern trading systems are undergoing a fundamental shift as financial institutions move from legacy infrastructure to cloud-native, data-driven architectures. This talk explores how database and DevOps practices enable high-performance trading platforms that process millions of events, risk scenarios, and market signals in real time.  We will walk through practical implementation patterns from real-world systems, including how teams reduced request-response latency by up to 80% and accelerated deployment cycles by 4× using automated CI/CD pipelines, containerized workloads, and infrastructure-as-code. The session will highlight how distributed databases, streaming pipelines, and elastic cloud infrastructure work together to handle burst workloads such as market open spikes and stress-testing simulations.  A key focus will be on data engineering and observability, including techniques for debugging latency issues, ensuring data consistency across services, and managing schema evolution in fast-moving environments. We will also cover the integration of machine learning pipelines into production systems, and how teams operationalize them reliably using MLOps practices.  Finally, the talk will share lessons learned—what worked, what failed, and what we would change—covering trade-offs in architecture, cost management, and reliability. Attendees will gain actionable insights into building scalable, resilient, and observable data platforms for real-time applications beyond finance.

Title: Centralized Data Lakes in Database DevOps: Performance and Governance
Abstract: With global data projected to reach 175 zettabytes, enterprises now manage 60% of the world’s data, exposing the scalability and governance limits of traditional warehouses. In data-intensive sectors, organizations process an average of 9.1 petabytes per enterprise, yet 76% struggle with data lake governance and 68% lack clear data ownership—leading to underutilized and unreliable data platforms.  This session presents a Database DevOps–aligned framework for centralized data lakes that delivers measurable gains in performance, cost efficiency, and governance. Mature implementations achieve 3.2× faster time-to-insight, 54% reduction in data preparation effort, and 59.3% lower storage costs. Multi-layered architectures improve query response times by 73%, increase resource utilization by 81%, and reduce bottlenecks by 67.4%, while distributed cloud storage supports 8.7× more concurrent users with 99.99% availability.  Beyond architecture, DevOps-driven governance and master data management reduce inconsistencies by 83.6% and increase data utilization by 412%. Security-by-design practices cut unauthorized access incidents by 78.4% and reduce compliance penalties by 85.4%.  Attendees will learn actionable strategies to operationalize centralized data lakes using automation, CI/CD for data pipelines, observability, and governance controls—transforming enterprise data platforms into scalable, secure, and high-performance systems.

Title: Operationalizing Semantic Consistency: A DevOps Framework for Reliable Cross-Engine Analytics
Abstract: Modern data platforms increasingly depend on multiple query engines to balance performance, scalability, and workload diversity. However, SQL compatibility alone does not guarantee consistent analytical outcomes. Variations in type handling, null semantics, aggregations, and optimizer behavior often introduce subtle inconsistencies, creating challenges for data reliability, testing, and governance in Database DevOps environments.  This session presents a practical, DevOps-oriented framework for enforcing semantic consistency across heterogeneous analytics systems. By introducing analytical intent as a first-class abstraction, we demonstrate how identical queries can produce divergent results across engines such as Spark, Trino, and Snowflake. We propose a canonical semantic layer that decouples logic from execution, enabling deterministic query translation and validation.  Additionally, we outline CI/CD-integrated validation workflows, semantic testing strategies, and observability patterns that help detect and prevent drift. Attendees will learn how to embed semantic guarantees into data pipelines, improving reproducibility, trust, and operational resilience in modern data platforms.

Title: Database DevOps for AI-Driven Enterprise Systems
Abstract: Modern enterprise platforms generate vast volumes of structured and semi-structured data across engineering, supply chain, and operational systems. As organizations embed AI into these environments, database reliability, governance, and deployment automation become mission-critical. This session explores how Database DevOps practices enable scalable, resilient data foundations that power intelligent enterprise workflows.  Using real-world product lifecycle ecosystems as a case study, the talk demonstrates how version-controlled schemas, automated migrations, CI/CD pipelines, and observability frameworks ensure high data integrity across PLM, ERP, and analytics platforms. Attendees will learn how to manage complex relational and distributed databases that support AI-driven anomaly detection, predictive cost modeling, and automated workflow orchestration.  The session also covers data governance strategies, performance optimization, rollback mechanisms, and environment parity across development, staging, and production. By integrating Database DevOps principles with AI-enabled systems, organizations can reduce deployment risk, improve data quality, and accelerate innovation while maintaining compliance and operational stability in large-scale enterprise environments.

Title: Modern Distributed Databases: Architecture, DevOps, and Operational Trade-offs
Abstract: As organizations scale cloud-native platforms, traditional monolithic databases struggle to meet the demands of high availability, global distribution, and real-time data processing. This session explores the evolution of distributed database architectures and the critical role DevOps practices play in building, deploying, and operating resilient data platforms at scale.  We will examine foundational concepts such as the CAP theorem, replication models, sharding strategies, and consensus protocols like Raft and Paxos that enable modern distributed databases to maintain availability, performance, and fault tolerance. The talk will also highlight how DevOps-driven automation through infrastructure as code, configuration management, and continuous deployment improves database reliability, scalability, and operational efficiency in production environments.  Additionally, the session will address modern security and governance practices including zero-trust infrastructure, certificate-based authentication, and distributed key management. Attendees will gain practical insights into managing distributed databases through DevOps methodologies, optimizing operational workflows, and building secure, scalable, and highly automated data platforms for modern enterprise and cloud-native applications.

Title: Intentional Delivery in AI-Driven Database DevOps
Abstract: AI-assisted development is transforming Database DevOps, shifting the core constraint from “can we build it?” to “should we build it this way, and will it deliver reliable outcomes at scale?” While AI accelerates schema design, query generation, pipeline automation, and operational tasks, speed without structure can introduce inconsistency, increase data risk, and erode trust—creating what can be understood as experience debt across systems and teams.  HAX (Human & AI Experience), conceptualized by Muthu and developed by Esperia, offers a structured approach to this shift. It integrates two complementary systems: HX (Human Experience), which preserves essential human judgment in areas like system design, data modeling, governance, and reasoning, and AX (AI Experience), which applies AI intentionally across six capability areas: detect, predict, scale, synthesize, execute, and ethics. Together, they ensure that automation enhances—not compromises—data integrity, performance, and reliability.  The framework operates through a six-stage delivery methodology spanning Discovery, Design, and Development, clarifying where human oversight is critical and where AI can safely accelerate tasks such as query optimization, migration workflows, and observability insights. HAX supports both lightweight adoption within existing DevOps pipelines and scaled implementation through reusable knowledge artifacts that standardize AI interactions across tools like ChatGPT and Gemini.

Title: Ethical DevOps for AI Data Pipelines: 286% Deployment Efficiency Gains
Abstract: As AI adoption accelerates, with 83% of enterprises fast-tracking integration, aligning DevOps practices with ethical AI has become critical in database and data pipeline environments. This session focuses on how teams integrate ethical validation directly into data and database DevOps workflows, and what results they achieved in real-world systems.  We walk through practical implementations where automated ethical checks are embedded into CI/CD pipelines handling data ingestion, transformation, and model deployment. These pipelines detected 89% of potential bias issues before release while reducing validation time by 56%. We also examine continuous monitoring setups across data and model layers that improved bias detection accuracy by 79% and increased regulatory compliance by 84%.  The talk highlights architecture and tooling decisions behind cloud-scale observability systems processing over 100,000 events per second, reducing incident response time by 78% and improving early issue detection by 91%. A healthcare data platform case study shows how these practices reduced patient data privacy incidents by 67% and increased clinician trust by 79%.  Attendees will learn how to design ethical validation into database DevOps pipelines, implement governance and monitoring workflows with measurable impact, and apply automation patterns that improved deployment efficiency by 286% and overall project success rates by 75%. The session emphasizes lessons learned, trade-offs, and practical approaches that teams can adopt in their own data systems.

Title: Securing Agentic AI Data Flows: Confidential MCP for Privacy-Safe Tooling in Database-Driven Systems
Abstract: As agentic AI systems increasingly interact with enterprise data platforms in healthcare, finance, and legal domains, a critical gap has emerged: large language models running inside Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs) cannot safely query external databases or services without risking sensitive data exposure. While confidential compute infrastructure has matured, the data access layer remains fundamentally insecure.  This talk introduces Confidential MCP (C-MCP), a backwards-compatible extension to Anthropic’s Model Context Protocol, designed to enable auditable, privacy-preserving tool and database access for LLM agents operating in secure environments. Building on architectures such as Meta’s WhatsApp Private Processing and validated confidential computing practices, C-MCP introduces a three-zone service topology, an Anonymization Transform Layer with entropy-bounded controls, and Attested Egress Policies that govern what data can leave secure enclaves.  We will explore why traditional MCP-based integrations fail in database-centric systems, highlighting challenges in transport security, dynamic schema and tool discovery, and authentication mismatches, and how C-MCP resolves them in production-like environments.  The session also addresses operational realities, including query overreach from smaller open-source models, cumulative data leakage risks in multi-step reasoning workflows, and performance overheads when integrating secure inference with real-time data systems.  Through real-world scenarios including clinical data pipelines governed by HIPAA, contract intelligence systems handling privileged information, and AML analytics platforms subject to GLBA and GDPR, attendees will learn how to design secure, compliant, and observable AI-driven data workflows with enforceable egress controls.

Title: AI-Driven Database DevOps: Transforming Enterprise Data Operations with 287% ROI and 42% Faster Incident Resolution
Abstract: Modern enterprises are managing increasingly complex database ecosystems spanning on-premises infrastructure, hybrid cloud environments, distributed platforms, and real-time data pipelines. Traditional operational models struggle to keep pace with rising performance demands, deployment velocity, and reliability expectations. This session presents comprehensive research into AI-driven Database DevOps strategies based on empirical analysis of more than 300 enterprise implementations and longitudinal operational studies across large-scale production environments. The presentation explores how artificial intelligence and automation are transforming database operations across four critical areas: intelligent performance monitoring, anomaly detection, predictive maintenance, and automated incident remediation. AI-powered monitoring platforms achieved 96% accuracy in identifying performance anomalies while processing massive telemetry streams across distributed database environments. Predictive analytics reduced unplanned downtime by more than 75% by identifying infrastructure and query performance issues days before service impact. Automated troubleshooting and root-cause analysis reduced mean time to resolution from 87 minutes to under 20 minutes, significantly improving operational resilience and service reliability. Attendees will learn how organizations implementing AI-enabled Database DevOps frameworks realized measurable business outcomes, including 34% productivity improvements across operations teams, 29% reductions in operational costs, and substantial reductions in alert fatigue while maintaining high critical-event detection accuracy. The research also demonstrates compelling financial impact, with organizations achieving an average 287% ROI over three years and accelerated payback periods through improved automation, operational efficiency, and reduced downtime. The session further examines the architectural foundations required for successful adoption, including observability pipelines, AI/ML operational models, automated governance frameworks, policy-driven remediation, and scalable deployment strategies. Practical implementation guidance will be shared for integrating AI into existing Database DevOps workflows while maintaining security, compliance, and operational stability. Designed for database engineers, DevOps leaders, SRE teams, architects, and technology executives, this session provides actionable insights into building intelligent, self-optimizing database operations capable of supporting modern enterprise-scale digital transformation initiatives.

Title: Cloud-Native Core Systems: Database & DevOps for Insurance
Abstract: Insurance organizations face growing pressure to modernize legacy core platforms as regulatory demands increase and digital competitors accelerate delivery cycles. Decades-old monolithic systems—particularly tightly coupled application and database layers—limit agility, slow releases, and struggle to scale during catastrophe-driven claims spikes.  This session examines how cloud-native engineering, combined with modern database and DevOps practices, transforms core insurance systems. We explore microservices architectures that decouple data domains, containerization and Kubernetes orchestration for high availability, and Infrastructure-as-Code for reproducible, resilient database and application environments.
